quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Greenhouse gas"?

options.text: Site designed for solid waste disposal with clay or synthetic liners, daily cover, and leachate collection systems
Liquid percolating through waste containing dissolved and suspended contaminants leaching to groundwater
Atmospheric gas (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, fluorine gases) absorbing infrared radiation and warming earth's surface
Reduced fitness in a population due to breeding among close relatives, increasing susceptibility to disease and environmental stress

quiz_questions.explanation: "Greenhouse gas" is defined as: Atmospheric gas (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, fluorine gases) absorbing infrared radiation and warming earth's surface. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Radiative forcing"?

options.text: The restriction of a species to a particular geographic area, increasing vulnerability to local environmental changes and extinction
Change in energy balance at top of atmosphere from human and natural factors, measured in watts per square meter (W/m²)
The total variety of genes and alleles within a population; higher diversity increases resilience to environmental change
A soil's ability to hold and exchange positively charged nutrients (Ca²⁺, K⁺, Mg²⁺), essential for plant nutrition

quiz_questions.explanation: "Radiative forcing" is defined as: Change in energy balance at top of atmosphere from human and natural factors, measured in watts per square meter (W/m²). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Global warming potential (GWP)"?

options.text: The limits within which a regulated population oscillates around carrying capacity, determined by regulatory mechanisms
Active process of intentionally restoring degraded ecosystems to a more natural or desired state
Relative warming power of greenhouse gas compared to CO₂ over 100-year time horizon (CH₄: 28–34, N₂O: 265)
Reduced fitness in a population due to breeding among close relatives, increasing susceptibility to disease and environmental stress

quiz_questions.explanation: "Global warming potential (GWP)" is defined as: Relative warming power of greenhouse gas compared to CO₂ over 100-year time horizon (CH₄: 28–34, N₂O: 265). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Climate sensitivity"?

options.text: Modern electricity system with digital communication, monitoring, and control enabling demand management and renewable integration
A large geographic area with characteristic climate, vegetation, and animal communities adapted to specific environmental conditions
The restriction of a species to a particular geographic area, increasing vulnerability to local environmental changes and extinction
Temperature increase from doubling atmospheric CO₂, estimated at 2.5–4°C based on paleoclimate and model evidence

quiz_questions.explanation: "Climate sensitivity" is defined as: Temperature increase from doubling atmospheric CO₂, estimated at 2.5–4°C based on paleoclimate and model evidence. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Tipping point"?

options.text: Total greenhouse gas emissions from extracting, processing, transporting, and burning a fuel
The average number of offspring a female is expected to produce for the rest of her life at a given age
Threshold in climate system beyond which change becomes irreversible or self-reinforcing (ice sheet collapse, Amazon dieback)
Liquid percolating through waste containing dissolved and suspended contaminants leaching to groundwater

quiz_questions.explanation: "Tipping point" is defined as: Threshold in climate system beyond which change becomes irreversible or self-reinforcing (ice sheet collapse, Amazon dieback). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Atmospheric gas (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, fluorine gases) absorbing infrared radiation and warming earth's surface"?

options.text: Biodiversity
Inhibition
Greenhouse gas
Doubling time

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Greenhouse gas".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Change in energy balance at top of atmosphere from human and natural factors, measured in watts per square meter (W/m²)"?

options.text: Radiative forcing
Erosion
Doubling time
Bioavailability

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Radiative forcing".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Relative warming power of greenhouse gas compared to CO₂ over 100-year time horizon (CH₄: 28–34, N₂O: 265)"?

options.text: Global warming potential (GWP)
Climate justice
Fishing down the food chain
Restoration intensity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Global warming potential (GWP)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Temperature increase from doubling atmospheric CO₂, estimated at 2.5–4°C based on paleoclimate and model evidence"?

options.text: Species richness
Secondary pollutant
Groundwater
Climate sensitivity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Climate sensitivity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Threshold in climate system beyond which change becomes irreversible or self-reinforcing (ice sheet collapse, Amazon dieback)"?

options.text: Tipping point
Density-dependent factor
Reproductive value
Soil acidification

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Tipping point".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases is accurate?

options.text: Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases result from accumulation of greenhouse gases (primarily carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide) trapping infrared radiation and warming the atmosphere at approximately 0.18°C per decade since 1981.

Mining and Land Degradation involve extraction of mineral and energy resources essential for modern society while causing severe environmental degradation including water pollution, habitat destruction, acid drainage, and waste accumulation...

Climate change effects include ecosystem disruption (species range shifts, phenological mismatches), agricultural impacts (yield reductions, new pests), public health threats (heat stress, vector-borne disease expansion), and infrastructure...

Water Pollution Types and Sources degrade drinking water quality, aquatic ecosystem integrity, and human health through point sources (factories, wastewater plants) and nonpoint sources (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater) introducing...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases?

options.text: Water Pollution Types and Sources degrade drinking water quality, aquatic ecosystem integrity, and human health through point sources (factories, wastewater plants) and nonpoint sources (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater) introducing...
Waste hierarchy emphasizes reduction, reuse, and recycling over landfilling and incineration, requiring collection infrastructure and market development for recovered materials.

Anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions from fossil fuel combustion (75%), agriculture (14%), and land use (11%) have increased atmospheric CO₂ from 315 ppm (1958) to 425 ppm (2024), exceeding pre-industrial levels by 50%.
Climate change is altering precipitation patterns and increasing water stress in many regions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases?

options.text: Soil composition—a mixture of minerals, organic matter, water, and gases—varies spatially reflecting parent material, climate, topography, time, and biological activity.
Successful restoration requires understanding the original ecosystem's composition, structure, and ecological processes, then removing limiting factors and facilitating species reestablishment.
Climate impacts include temperature extremes, precipitation changes, sea-level rise, and ecosystem disruptions affecting food, water, and health security.

Air Pollution Sources and Types comprise particulate matter, gaseous pollutants (nitrogen oxides, sulfur dioxide, ozone, carbon monoxide), and heavy metals causing respiratory disease, cardiovascular impacts, and premature mortality affecting...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases?

options.text: Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases result from accumulation of greenhouse gases (primarily carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide) trapping infrared radiation and warming the atmosphere at approximately 0.18°C per decade since 1981.
Waste hierarchy emphasizes reduction, reuse, and recycling over landfilling and incineration, requiring collection infrastructure and market development for recovered materials.

Human Population Growth has accelerated from 1 billion (1800) to 8 billion (2023), driven primarily by mortality reduction while fertility rates lag in developing countries, creating a population momentum continuing growth to 9–10 billion by...

Exploitation of fossil fuels has powered industrialization and modern civilization but has extracted resources representing millions of years of photosynthesis, increasing atmospheric carbon dioxide from 280 ppm to 425 ppm.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases?

options.text: Climate change is altering precipitation patterns and increasing water stress in many regions.
Nutrient Cycles move essential elements including carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur between the abiotic environment and living organisms in indefinitely recycling biogeochemical pathways.

Anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions from fossil fuel combustion (75%), agriculture (14%), and land use (11%) have increased atmospheric CO₂ from 315 ppm (1958) to 425 ppm (2024), exceeding pre-industrial levels by 50%.

Resource depletion reflects overconsumption in wealthy nations (approximately 2.7 global hectares per person on average, but ~8 gha in the United States and other high-consumption nations) and accelerating extraction in developing countries.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases?

options.text: Preserving crop genetic diversity in seed banks to maintain disease resistance alleles and adaptability for future climate scenarios
Using succession principles in invasive species management by understanding how native species can reestablish and outcompete invasives
Specifying low-VOC paints, adhesives, and furnishings in building design to minimize indoor chemical exposure and sick building syndrome
Measuring atmospheric CO₂ at observation stations (Mauna Loa, South Pole) to track accumulation and verify emission reduction policies

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases?

options.text: Calculating corporate carbon footprints across supply chains to identify reduction opportunities and guide sustainability initiatives
Managing breeding programs for endangered California condors by carefully tracking genetic diversity and preventing inbreeding depression
Protecting coral reef ecosystems through MPAs that prevent fishing-related habitat destruction and allow reef fish recovery
Managing forest successional pathways after logging or fire by controlling which species regenerate to achieve desired forest composition

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Restoring mined lands by deliberately introducing pioneer species and managing succession toward productive ecosystems or wildlife habitat
Determining how disease (plague) limits prairie dog colonies and designing conservation strategies accounting for disease as a critical factor
Using PVA models for Florida panther recovery to determine required population growth rates and habitat connectivity for species recovery
Projecting future climate scenarios using Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP1-2.6 through SSP5-8.5) from IPCC AR6 to inform national climate policies and adaptation planning

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases?

options.text: Designing vehicle fuel economy standards and incentivizing adoption of efficient vehicles to reduce transportation energy demand
Managing harmful algal blooms (cyanobacteria) in freshwater lakes through nutrient reduction and copper sulfate applications in emergency situations
Genetic testing of wolf populations in northern Rocky Mountains to monitor genetic connectivity and guide wildlife corridor management
Measuring atmospheric CO₂ at observation stations (Mauna Loa, South Pole) to track accumulation and verify emission reduction policies

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases in practice?

options.text: Establishing extended producer responsibility (EPR) programs making manufacturers responsible for product end-of-life management, incentivizing design for recycling
Modeling sea-level rise impacts on coastal cities and small island nations, informing relocation planning and adaptation infrastructure investment
Calculating corporate carbon footprints across supply chains to identify reduction opportunities and guide sustainability initiatives
Controlling invasive carp populations in North American lakes through manipulation of recruitment drivers and commercial harvesting

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Warming and Greenhouse Gases.
